name: The Periodic Table
subject: Chemistry

sections.title: I. History & Organisation
sections.subtitle: Groups, periods, Mendeleev
paragraphs: The periodic table is one of the most important tools in chemistry. It organises all 118 known elements by their atomic number, electron configuration, and recurring chemical properties. Every element that exists — from the hydrogen in water to the gold in jewellery — has its place on this table.

In 1869, Russian chemist Dmitri Mendeleev published the first widely recognised periodic table. His genius was not just in arranging the elements — it was in leaving deliberate gaps where elements should exist but had not yet been discovered. He predicted the properties of gallium, scandium, and germanium years before they were found in nature. When gallium was discovered in 1875, its density, melting point, and atomic mass matched Mendeleev's prediction almost exactly.

The modern periodic table arranges elements in order of increasing atomic number — the number of protons in the nucleus. Elements in the same column, called a group, share similar chemical properties because they have the same number of valence electrons. Valence electrons are the electrons in the outermost shell — the ones that participate in chemical bonding.

The table is divided into 18 groups (vertical columns) and 7 periods (horizontal rows). As you move left to right across a period, atomic number increases by exactly one each step. As you move down a group, atoms get progressively larger because each new period adds a complete new electron shell around the nucleus.

diagram.title: Structure of the Periodic Table
diagram.description: Simplified periodic table grid showing groups (columns), periods (rows), and the d-block transition metals gap. Arrows indicate: atomic number increases left to right, each row adds an electron shell.
diagram.interactiveHint: Tap any region to see details

video.title: CrashCourse Chemistry — The Periodic Table
video.description: Mendeleev's story, how the table is organised, groups and periods explained visually

callout.content: Mendeleev was so confident in his system that he publicly predicted three missing elements in 1871. He called them eka-boron, eka-aluminium, and eka-silicon. Within 15 years all three were discovered — scandium, gallium, and germanium — and every property matched his predictions.

checkpoint.question: As you move left to right across a period, what happens to the atomic number?
options: It decreases by one each step
It stays the same throughout the period
It increases by one each step
It doubles with each new element
checkpoint.onCorrect: Correct — each step right adds exactly one proton to the nucleus. Section II now unlocked. Progress: 25%.
checkpoint.onWrong: Not quite — atomic number = number of protons, and each element has exactly one more proton than the last. Try again.

mild.description: 5 cards covering groups, periods, atomic number.

moderate.description: Drag elements into correct period bucket. Elements: H, Li, Na, K, Rb.

severe.description: App speaks: 'Say the atomic number of Hydrogen.' Then: 'Say the atomic number of Carbon.'

sections.recapOnReturn: You were learning: the periodic table has 18 groups and 7 periods, arranged by increasing atomic number.

sections.title: II. Element Groups & Families
sections.subtitle: Metals, halogens, nobles
paragraphs: Elements are grouped into families based on their chemical behaviour. The most important families to know are: alkali metals (Group 1), alkaline earth metals (Group 2), transition metals (Groups 3-12), halogens (Group 17), and noble gases (Group 18). Each family has a distinctive set of properties that its members share.

Alkali metals — lithium, sodium, potassium, rubidium, caesium, and francium — are the most reactive metals on the table. They all have exactly one valence electron which they give away easily in reactions. Sodium reacts violently with water to produce sodium hydroxide and hydrogen gas. Critically, the reaction gets more violent as you move down the group — potassium ignites spontaneously, rubidium explodes. The outer electron gets further from the nucleus and is held less tightly, making it easier to lose.

Noble gases occupy Group 18 and are almost completely unreactive. Helium, neon, argon, krypton, xenon, and radon all have completely full outer electron shells. With no tendency to gain or lose electrons, they have no reason to form bonds. This is why neon signs glow without the neon reacting with the glass, the electricity, or the air around it — neon is chemically indifferent to everything around it.

Halogens in Group 17 are the polar opposite of noble gases. They are the most reactive non-metals because their outer shells need just one more electron to become full. Fluorine is so reactive it corrodes through glass. Chlorine is used to kill bacteria in swimming pools. Iodine is essential for the human thyroid gland to function. Same group, same electron need, completely different applications in the real world.

Transition metals fill the central block (Groups 3-12). Unlike the main group elements, transition metals have partially filled d-orbitals which give them unusual properties — they form coloured compounds, they can exist in multiple oxidation states, and they make excellent catalysts. Iron, copper, gold, silver, titanium, and platinum are all transition metals. Most of the metals you encounter in everyday life come from this block.

diagram.title: Element Families — Colour-Coded Periodic Table
diagram.description: Full periodic table with each family highlighted: Group 1 (Red - Alkali metals), Group 2 (Orange - Alkaline earth), Groups 3-12 (Blue - Transition metals), Group 17 (Pink - Halogens), Group 18 (Teal - Noble gases).
diagram.interactiveHint: Tap any coloured region to see: family name, group number, valence electrons, reactivity, 3 examples

video.title: Tyler DeWitt — Families on the Periodic Table
video.description: Alkali metals, halogens, noble gases in plain conversational English

driftVideo.title: Periodic Videos — Sodium in Water
driftVideo.description: Sodium metal dropped into water — violent reaction, ignition, explosion. Shows exactly what the student was reading about when they drifted.

callout.content: You use two halogens every single morning before you leave the house. The fluoride in your toothpaste is a fluorine compound — it strengthens tooth enamel. The chloride in your table salt is a chlorine compound — sodium chloride, NaCl. Two of the most reactive non-metals on the table, made completely safe through bonding with metals.

checkpoint.question: Why are noble gases almost completely unreactive?
options: They are too large to form bonds with other atoms
They are gases so chemical reactions cannot occur
Their outer electron shells are completely full
They are only found in the upper atmosphere
checkpoint.onCorrect: Correct — a full outer shell means no drive to gain or lose electrons. No electron transfer, no reaction. Section III now unlocked. Progress: 50%.
checkpoint.onWrong: Not quite — think about what makes an element reactive. It is about electrons, not size or state of matter.

mild.description: 5 cards on element families. Covers all major families.

moderate.description: Tap Period 2 elements in atomic number order: Li Be B C N O F Ne.

severe.description: Sodium in water video (1 min). Then voice: 'What products form when sodium reacts with water?'

sections.recapOnReturn: You were learning: noble gases are unreactive because their outer electron shells are completely full.

sections.title: III. Periodic Trends
sections.subtitle: Radius, ionisation, reactivity
paragraphs: Periodic trends are predictable patterns in element properties that repeat as you move across a period or down a group. Understanding why these trends exist means you can predict the behaviour of any element on the table without memorising individual facts. There are four main trends: atomic radius, ionisation energy, electronegativity, and reactivity.

Atomic radius is the size of an atom — measured as half the distance between two identical bonded atoms. Atomic radius decreases as you move left to right across a period. This seems counterintuitive — you are adding more electrons, so why does the atom shrink? The answer is nuclear charge. Each step right adds one more proton to the nucleus. More protons means a stronger pull on the entire electron cloud, drawing it closer to the centre.

Atomic radius increases as you move down a group. Each new period adds a complete new electron shell between the nucleus and the outermost electrons. More shells means more distance. Lithium has 2 shells. Sodium has 3. Potassium has 4. Each step down Group 1 adds a whole new layer — the atom grows.

Ionisation energy is the energy required to remove one electron from a gaseous atom. It follows the opposite trend to atomic radius. Ionisation energy increases left to right across a period — more nuclear charge holds electrons more tightly. Ionisation energy decreases down a group — outer electrons are further from the nucleus and shielded by inner shells.

Electronegativity measures how strongly an atom attracts electrons in a chemical bond. Fluorine is the most electronegative element on the table. Electronegativity increases left to right across a period and decreases down a group — the same pattern as ionisation energy. Francium is the least electronegative. Fluorine is the most.

diagram.title: How Trends Move Across the Table
diagram.description: Periodic table outline with four trend arrows: Atomic radius decreases right (red), increases down (red). Ionisation energy increases right (teal), decreases down (teal). Plus atomic radius comparison: Li(152pm) → Na(186pm) → K(227pm) → Rb(248pm) → Cs(265pm).
diagram.interactiveHint: Tap any arrow for a detailed explanation

video.title: Khan Academy — Periodic Table Trends
video.description: Atomic radius and ionisation energy trends explained visually with arrows

callout.content: Atomic radius and ionisation energy always move in opposite directions. When atomic radius is high (bottom-left), ionisation energy is low. When atomic radius is low (top-right), ionisation energy is high. You never need to memorise both — just remember they are always opposites.

checkpoint.question: Magnesium (Mg) is in Period 3, Group 2. Aluminium (Al) is in Period 3, Group 13. Which has a larger atomic radius?
options: Magnesium — it is further left in the period
They are the same — same period, same size
Aluminium — higher group number means larger
checkpoint.onCorrect: Correct — moving right across Period 3, nuclear charge increases and pulls the electron cloud inward. Magnesium is further left so its radius is larger. Section IV now unlocked. Progress: 75%.
checkpoint.onWrong: Not quite — same period means same number of electron shells. The difference is nuclear charge. More protons pulls electrons closer.

mild.description: 5 cards on trend directions across all four major trends.

moderate.description: Blank periodic table — drag four arrows to show correct direction of trends.

severe.description: Draw and label trend arrows from memory on blank periodic table.

sections.recapOnReturn: You were learning: atomic radius decreases left to right — nuclear charge pulls the electron cloud inward.

sections.title: IV. Reading the Periodic Table
sections.subtitle: Tiles, config, isotopes
paragraphs: Every element tile on the periodic table contains four pieces of information: the atomic number, the element symbol, the element name, and the atomic mass. Reading these correctly is not just a skill for exams — it is the foundation of every chemistry calculation you will ever do.

The atomic number is always a whole number at the top of the tile. It tells you exactly how many protons are in the nucleus. For a neutral atom it also tells you the number of electrons. Carbon has atomic number 6: six protons in the nucleus, six electrons orbiting it. Change the proton count and you have a different element entirely.

The atomic mass shown on the tile is not a whole number — it is the weighted average mass of all naturally occurring isotopes. Carbon shows 12.011, not 12, because about 1.1% of all carbon atoms in nature are carbon-13 — one neutron heavier than common carbon-12.

The electron configuration can be read directly from an element's position for the first 20 elements. The period number tells you how many electron shells are occupied. The group number tells you how many electrons are in the outermost shell. Sodium sits in Period 3, Group 1 — 3 shells, 1 electron in the outermost. Configuration: 2,8,1. Total: 11 electrons. Atomic number: 11. They always match.

Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons. Carbon-12 has 6 protons and 6 neutrons. Carbon-13 has 6 protons and 7 neutrons. Carbon-14 has 6 protons and 8 neutrons. All three are carbon — same atomic number, same chemical behaviour — but different masses.

diagram.title: Reading an Element Tile (Carbon)
diagram.description: Large element tile for Carbon with labelled arrows: 6 → Atomic number (protons), C → Symbol, Carbon → Name, 12.011 → Atomic mass (weighted average). Plus Sodium electron shell diagram: three concentric circles with 2, 8, 1 electrons.
diagram.interactiveHint: Tap blurred tiles to fill in missing information

video.title: Professor Dave Explains — Reading the Periodic Table Tile
video.description: Walks through one complete element tile — atomic number, symbol, name, mass

callout.content: Many students confuse atomic number with atomic mass. Remember: atomic number is always a whole number — it counts protons. Atomic mass is almost never a whole number — it is a weighted average of isotopes. In an exam, if you see a decimal it is always the atomic mass. If you see a whole number it is always the atomic number.

checkpoint.question: Sodium has atomic number 11 and sits in Period 3, Group 1. What is its electron configuration?
checkpoint.onCorrect: Correct — Period 3 means 3 shells. Shell 1 holds 2, shell 2 holds 8, shell 3 holds 1. Total = 11 = atomic number. Lesson complete! Progress: 100%.
checkpoint.onWrong: Not quite — count the shells first (period number = number of shells), then fill them in order: 2 then 8 then the remainder.

mild.description: 5 cards on reading element tiles — atomic number, mass, symbol, configuration.

moderate.description: Blank element tile with four slots. Drag correct info into each slot for Carbon, Oxygen, Sodium.

severe.description: App speaks: 'Oxygen has atomic number 8. How many electrons does a neutral oxygen atom have?' Then: 'Sodium is in Period 3, Group 1. Say its electron configuration.'

sections.recapOnReturn: You were learning: atomic number = protons = electrons in a neutral atom. Always a whole number. Never the same as atomic mass.

curiosityBomb: If you could line up every atom in your body side by side, the line would stretch about 2 light-years — halfway to the nearest star. Yet 99% of your body is made of just 6 elements from the periodic table: oxygen, carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, calcium, and phosphorus.
